{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{Location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Shōwa era (Japan)|ja|承和}} (834–848). == Enshrined Deities == * **Main Hall**: ** {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ** {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ** {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * **Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)**: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * **Wakamiya Shrine**: ** Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * **Tagorihime-no-Mikoto** (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}) * **Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto** (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島}}) * **Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto** (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * **Ōita Prefectural Historic Site** (March 30, 1976) * **Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument** for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * **February 11**: Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * **April 21**: Annual Festival * **Third Saturday and Sunday in September**: Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[ja:薦神社]]

A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[ja:薦神社]]

{{Shrine | Name = Komo Shrine | image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[ja:薦神社]]

{{Shrine | Name = Komo Shrine | image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:薦神社]]

For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Komo Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:薦神社]]

Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Komo Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:薦神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Komo Shrine | image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Komo Shrine | image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Japan)]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Historic Sites in Ōita Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Natural Monuments in Ōita Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[category:unsorted Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | Coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (Annual Festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': Annual Festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo {{Shrine | Name = Komo Shrine | image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate Name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita {{Shrine | name = Komo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] needing attribution]] [[Category:Edo Period architecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:17th-century architecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Nakatsu, Ōita]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Komo Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:薦神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Komo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Pages needing revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Komo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Pages needing revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Komo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Komo Shrine (Oita)-qq]] [[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Komo Shrine (Oita)-qq]] [[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Komo Shrine (Oita)-qq]] [[Category:Komo Shrine (Oita)]][[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Komo Shrine (Oita)-qq]] [[Category:Komo Shrine {{Shrine | name = Komo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Komo.jpg|280px|Main Gate]]
Shinmon (Sacred Gate) | Address = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, Ōaza Osada 209 | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}
{{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比売大神}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} | Status = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] (県社) | Established = [[Shōwa era (Japan)|Shōwa era]] (9th century) | Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Alternate name = Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮) | Festivals = April 21 (festival) | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=2.2|lon_deg=131|lon_min=13|lon_sec=5.1|position=top|width=210|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|34|2.2|N|131|13|5.1|E}}}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (薦神社) is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} located in {{ill|Nakatsu, Ōita|ja|中津市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. It is also referred to as the Ōsada Hachiman Shrine (大貞八幡宮). The shrine features a sacred triangular pond, Misumi Pond (三角池, also historically written as 御澄池), representing the inner shrine, while the sacred hall is considered the outer shrine. This ancient shrine is said to be the progenitor of {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}, the primary shrine of all Hachiman shrines in Japan, with origins dating to the {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period|lt=Jōwa era|ja|承和}} (834–848). For this reason it is often called "Moto-Usa" == Enshrined Deities == * '''Main Hall''': ''' {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Hachiman Ōkami) ''' {{ill|Hime-no-Kami|ja|比咩大神}} (The Three Goddesses of Munakata) ''' {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|息長帯比売命}} * '''Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine)''': ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} (Susanoo-no-Mikoto) * '''Wakamiya Shrine''': ''' Offspring of Emperor Ōjin Hime-no-Kami refers to the {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|ja|宗像三女神}}, represented by: * '''Tagorihime-no-Mikoto''' (沖津宮 at {{ill|Okinoshima|ja|沖ノ島|en}}) * '''Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (中津宮 at {{ill|Ōshima (Fukuoka)|ja|大島_(福岡県)|fa|اوشیما (استان فوکوئوکا)|ceb|Ō_Shima_(pulo_sa_Hapon,_Fukuoka-ken)}}) * '''Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto''' (辺津宮 at {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像市田島}}). According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 1, Kami Age, Chapter 6, Variation 3, the Three Goddesses were said to have descended at Usa Island in Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, marking their connection to this sacred site. == Shrine Crest == The shrine uses the "Single Tomoe" crest (一つ巴). Unlike the "Triple Tomoe" of Usa Shrine, the Single Tomoe indicates Komo Shrine's status as a progenitor shrine. Some scholars suggest that the shrine originally enshrined one of the Three Goddesses, possibly Tatsutsuhime-no-Mikoto, rather than all three. == History == In {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 4 (720), during the {{ill|Hayato Rebellion|ja|隼人の反乱}}, the forces led by {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and Usa's "Divine Army," under Karashima Haduome, used sacred reed mats (薦枕, Komomakura) from Misumi Pond as a divine artifact to quell the rebellion. The Komokari Festival commemorates this event and is still performed every six years as part of the {{ill|Usa Shrine Procession|ja|宇佐神宮行幸会}} by the Karashima clan. Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo Shrine] {{Shinto navbox}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Komo Shrine (Oita)-qq]] [[Category:Komo Shrine Komo Shrine and Misumi Pond were designated as: * '''Ōita Prefectural Historic Site''' (March 30, 1976) * '''Ōita Prefectural Natural Monument''' for its aquatic and wetland plant communities. The Shinmon Gate, constructed by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 8 (1622), is a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. The shrine buildings date to the late Edo period. == Annual Rituals == * '''February 11''': Chinyoku Festival and Goshinkō-e (Demon Banishing Ceremony) * '''April 21''': festival * '''Third Saturday and Sunday in September''': Nakashū Festival (Sacred Procession) == Access == * From {{ill|Kokura Station|ja|小倉駅}}: Take the {{ill|Sonic (train)|ja|ソニック}}, a limited express train on the {{ill|Nippō Main Line|ja|日豊本線}}, to {{ill|Nakatsu Station|ja|中津駅}} (approximately 30 minutes). A 15-minute drive from the station. * By Bus: From Nakatsu Station South Exit, take a {{ill|Daiwa Kotsu|ja|大交北部バス}} bound for Tanaka or Shōnen Gakuin-Kansha-mae and alight at "Komo Shrine" (approximately 20 minutes). * By Car: Approximately 15 minutes from the Nakatsu IC on the {{ill|Nippō Expressway|ja|日豊自動車道}}. == References == * ''Photographic Collection: Komo Shrine, Shinmon Volume,'' Komo Culture Research Institute, 1999 * Kenichi Tanigawa (Ed.), ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites, Volume 1: Kyūshū,'' Hakusuisha, 1984, ISBN 4-560-02211-9 * ''Cultural Properties of Ōita Prefecture,'' Ōita Prefectural Board of Education, 1991 == See Also == * {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} * {{ill|Daigo Hisa|ja|大悟法久志}} == External Links == * [http://www.komojinja.jp/ Official Website of Komo
